CLINICAL INDICATION: 34-year-old male with sudden onset left-sided chest pain and dyspnea. No known prior lung disease. Rule out pneumothorax.

TECHNIQUE: Upright PA chest radiograph.

FINDINGS:

Lungs: There is a left-sided pneumothorax with a visible visceral pleural line identified in the left upper lobe. The lung apex is partially collapsed with an estimated volume loss of approximately 10–12%. No contralateral pleural abnormality is identified. The right lung demonstrates clear lung fields without focal consolidation, pleural effusion, or pneumothorax.

Pleura: No pleural effusion identified on the left. No pleural thickening.

Mediastinum: The mediastinum is midline without tracheal deviation. The cardiac silhouette is normal in size and contour. No pneumomediastinum.

Chest wall and osseous structures: No rib fractures identified. No subcutaneous emphysema. The visualized bony thorax is otherwise unremarkable.

Diaphragm: Both hemidiaphragms are well-defined and at normal level.

Other: No prior chest tube tracts identified. No acute osseous abnormality.

IMPRESSION:

1. Small left apical pneumothorax with estimated volume loss of 10–12%. No mediastinal shift or tension physiology.
2. No associated rib fractures or subcutaneous emphysema to suggest traumatic etiology; findings are most consistent with primary spontaneous pneumothorax in this young male patient.
3. Right lung is clear without evidence of contralateral pneumothorax or significant pulmonary pathology.
4. Clinical correlation recommended; close interval follow-up imaging suggested to assess for stability or progression.

CLINICAL INDICATION: 24-year-old male presenting with sudden onset left-sided chest pain and dyspnea. No history of trauma. Evaluate for pneumothorax.

Pleural spaces: Trace left apical pleural air as described. No pleural effusion bilaterally.

Mediastinum: The mediastinum is midline without shift. The cardiac silhouette is normal in size and contour. The aortic knob is well-defined.

Hila: Bilateral hilar contours are within normal limits without adenopathy or vascular prominence.

Chest wall and soft tissues: No subcutaneous emphysema. No rib fractures identified on this projection. No prior chest tube tracts or surgical changes are visible.

Osseous structures: Thoracic spine and visible bony structures are unremarkable. No acute fracture or lytic lesion identified.

Diaphragm and subdiaphragmatic region: Hemidiaphragms are well-defined and symmetrically positioned. No free subdiaphragmatic air.

1. Small left apical pneumothorax with an estimated visceral pleural line distance of approximately 1.5 cm and less than 15% volume loss. Findings are consistent with primary spontaneous pneumothorax in this young male patient.
2. No mediastinal shift or tension physiology.
3. Right lung clear. No associated rib fractures or subcutaneous emphysema.
4. Clinical correlation and close interval follow-up radiograph recommended to assess for stability or progression.

CLINICAL INDICATION: 34-year-old male presenting with acute onset right-sided pleuritic chest pain and dyspnea. History of prior spontaneous pneumothorax on the left. Evaluate for recurrent pneumothorax.

TECHNIQUE: Upright PA and lateral chest radiographs obtained.

FINDINGS:

Lungs: There is a moderate right-sided pneumothorax with a clearly visible visceral pleural line identified in the right mid and upper lung zones. The right lung demonstrates partial collapse with an estimated 30–35% volume loss. No tension physiology is identified; the mediastinum remains midline. The right costophrenic angle is sharp with no definitive pneumothorax component extending to the base. The left lung is clear without focal consolidation, effusion, or recurrent pneumothorax. Pleural thickening is noted at the left apex, consistent with the patient's reported prior left-sided pneumothorax and likely sequela of prior pleurodesis or spontaneous adhesion formation.

Heart and mediastinum: Cardiac silhouette is normal in size and configuration. The mediastinum is midline without shift. The trachea is midline.

Osseous structures and soft tissues: No acute rib fractures are identified. No subcutaneous emphysema is detected in the soft tissues of the chest wall or neck. The visualized osseous structures are otherwise unremarkable.

Other: No pneumoperitoneum. Visualized upper abdominal structures are unremarkable.

IMPRESSION:

1. Moderate right-sided spontaneous pneumothorax with estimated 30–35% volume loss. No tension physiology.
2. Left apical pleural thickening consistent with sequelae of prior left-sided pneumothorax.
3. Clinical and subspecialty surgical consultation recommended given moderate size and prior contralateral pneumothorax history.

CLINICAL INDICATION: 34-year-old male presenting to the emergency department following a high-speed motor vehicle collision with chest wall tenderness and decreased breath sounds on the right. Evaluate for traumatic injury.

TECHNIQUE: Portable AP radiograph of the chest obtained in the supine position.

FINDINGS:

A moderate right-sided pneumothorax is present, with a visible pleural line identified at the mid-lung zone and estimated 30–35% ipsilateral lung volume loss. The right lung demonstrates partial collapse with increased radiolucency in the lateral pleural space. No mediastinal shift is identified to suggest tension physiology at this time.

Subcutaneous emphysema is present along the right lateral chest wall and extends into the soft tissues of the right axilla. Multiple right-sided rib fractures are identified involving the posterior aspects of ribs 5, 6, and 7, with mild cortical offset and without significant displacement. A small focus of adjacent pleural thickening or hemorrhage cannot be excluded at the right base.

The left lung is clear and well expanded without focal consolidation, pleural effusion, or pneumothorax. The cardiac silhouette is normal in size. The mediastinum is midline. The osseous structures of the left hemithorax are intact. Endotracheal tube tip is not present; no central venous catheter or other support devices are identified.

IMPRESSION:

1. Moderate right-sided pneumothorax with approximately 30–35% volume loss, without radiographic evidence of tension physiology on this supine projection. Clinical correlation is recommended, as supine technique may underestimate pneumothorax size.
2. Right posterior rib fractures involving ribs 5 through 7, consistent with the reported traumatic mechanism.
3. Subcutaneous emphysema along the right lateral chest wall and axilla, likely related to the rib fractures and pneumothorax.
4. Small right basilar pleural density, possibly representing early hemothorax or pleural thickening; close interval follow-up or CT chest recommended for further characterization.
5. Left lung clear and expanded.

CLINICAL INDICATION: 34-year-old male with sudden onset left-sided chest pain and dyspnea. No history of trauma. Evaluate for pneumothorax.

TECHNIQUE: Upright PA chest radiograph.

FINDINGS:

Lungs: There is a large left-sided pneumothorax with near-complete collapse of the left lung, which is retracted toward the left hilum. The visceral pleural line is clearly delineated, and no lung markings are identified peripheral to this line. The estimated volumetric loss exceeds 60%. There is leftward displacement of the trachea and marked shift of the mediastinum to the left, with the cardiac silhouette displaced substantially toward the left hemithorax. The right hemidiaphragm is depressed and flattened. The right lung demonstrates compensatory hyperinflation with increased lucency but no focal consolidation, nodule, or pleural effusion on the right.

Pleural/Chest Wall: No rib fractures identified. No subcutaneous emphysema is detected at the chest wall soft tissues. No pleural thickening or calcification. No prior chest tube tracts are identified.

Mediastinum: Significant leftward mediastinal shift as described, raising concern for tension physiology. Cardiac silhouette is displaced but the overall cardiac contour is difficult to assess given the shift. No pneumomediastinum identified.

Bones and Soft Tissues: No acute osseous abnormality. Soft tissues are unremarkable.

IMPRESSION:

1. Large left-sided pneumothorax with greater than 60% estimated volume loss and significant leftward mediastinal shift, consistent with tension pneumothorax. Urgent clinical correlation and immediate decompression are recommended.
2. Compensatory hyperinflation of the right lung without additional acute parenchymal abnormality.
3. No rib fractures or subcutaneous emphysema identified. Findings favor a spontaneous etiology, though iatrogenic or occult traumatic causes cannot be excluded clinically.

CLINICAL INDICATION: 34-year-old male with acute onset right-sided pleuritic chest pain and dyspnea. History of Marfan syndrome. Evaluate for pneumothorax.

TECHNIQUE: Upright PA chest radiograph.

FINDINGS:

Lungs: There is a moderate right-sided pneumothorax with an estimated 35–40% volume loss. A distinct visceral pleural line is identified, with absence of lung markings peripheral to this line extending from the right apex to the mid-lung zone. The right lung demonstrates partial collapse with the right lower lobe remaining aerated. No contralateral pneumothorax is identified. The left lung is clear without focal consolidation, effusion, or mass. Mild bilateral apical pleural thickening is noted, likely representing underlying bullous disease, more prominent on the right.

Pleura: No pleural effusion identified bilaterally. No subcutaneous emphysema along the chest wall.

Mediastinum and Hila: The trachea and mediastinum remain midline without evidence of shift, arguing against tension physiology. Cardiac silhouette is mildly elongated and narrow, consistent with the patient's body habitus and known Marfan syndrome. Hilar structures are unremarkable.

Osseous Structures: No rib fractures identified. The thoracic cage is elongated and narrow. No acute osseous abnormality.

Soft Tissues: No subcutaneous emphysema. Soft tissues are otherwise unremarkable.

IMPRESSION:

1. Moderate right-sided pneumothorax with approximately 35–40% estimated volume loss. No mediastinal shift to suggest tension physiology at this time.
2. Bilateral apical pleural thickening with likely underlying apical bullous disease, more conspicuous on the right, consistent with the predisposing etiology in the setting of Marfan syndrome.
3. Elongated, narrow cardiac and thoracic configuration consistent with known Marfan habitus.

Clinical correlation and urgent surgical/pulmonary consultation recommended. Interval chest radiograph following any intervention is advised.

Findings were communicated directly to the clinical team at the time of interpretation.
